title: After a domain cutover, agent misdiagnoses stale local-resolver responses as a broken server config
summary: curl uses the system resolver; a router/ISP cache holding pre-cutover records makes a finished migration look like a failed deploy, sending the agent into pointless server-side churn.
mistake: After switching a custom domain to a new host (e.g. Cloudflare Pages), plain `curl https://www.example.com` kept returning the OLD host's error page (Vercel 402 DEPLOYMENT_DISABLED). The agent assumed the new DNS records or the Pages custom-domain binding were wrong and burned time re-creating DNS records, polling for 'propagation', and re-checking the hosting config. The real cause: the local WiFi router / ISP resolver still cached pre-cutover records (old CNAME to the previous host) from before a nameserver change. The authoritative NS and public resolvers (Cloudflare, Google) had been correct the whole time — curl simply asks the system resolver, so local checks do not represent global state.
instead: Verify a DNS/domain cutover with three checks before touching server config: (1) authoritative NS directly: `dig www.example.com @<assigned-ns>`, (2) a public resolver: `dig www.example.com @one.one.one.one`, (3) the new edge directly, bypassing DNS: `curl --resolve www.example.com:443:<edge-ip> https://www.example.com/`. If all three are correct, the migration is done and any local anomaly is resolver cache (wait for TTL; `resolvectl query`/`resolvectl flush-caches` on Linux only fixes the local layer, not the router's). Use `curl -v` and read the 'Connected to' line to see which origin IP actually answered — a response header like `server: Vercel` vs `server: cloudflare` identifies the layer instantly.
